message:
feat(mvcc,pager): fix DRO adversarial test assertions and refactor H11 condvar-drop fault hook

Tighten SSI abort policy tests: replace fragile recovery-contraction
assertion with a sliding-window-aware radius check, rewrite storm
suppression to compare CVaR penalties at different contention levels
instead of raw abort counts, and add a falsifiable DRO generation
counter. Refactor H11 fault hook so publish_completed_epoch() always
stores the epoch before optionally suppressing the Condvar notify,
clarifying that waiters see the epoch via timeout recheck not a hang.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

No content lost.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1try","depends_on_id":"bd-3kp","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-08T21:54:22Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":578,"issue_id":"bd-1try","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## §21 Risk Register + Open Questions + Future Work

### Risk Register (§21.0)
**R1. SSI abort rate too high:** Refine witness keys page→(page,range/cell). Safe snapshot for read-only. Intent-level rebase reduces 30-60%. PostgreSQL ~0.5% at row level; page coarser but merge compensates.
**R2. RaptorQ overhead dominates CPU:** Symbol sizing per object type. Aggressive cache. Profile hot paths (one lever per change).
**R3. Append-only storage grows:** Checkpoint, GC, compaction first-class. Budget enforcement. GC horizon = min(active begin_seq) bounds chain length.
**R4. Bootstrap chicken-and-egg:** Self-describing symbol records. One tiny mutable root pointer. Rebuild-from-scan fallback.
**R5. Multi-process MVCC complexity:** SHM protocol specified (§5.6.1). Lease-based TxnSlot cleanup. Phase 6 validates both in-process and cross-process.
**R6. File format compat vs "do it right":** Compatibility Mode = standard format. Native Mode = innovation. Conformance = observable behavior.
**R7. Mergeable writes correctness minefield:** Strict merge ladder (§5.10.4). Proptest + DPOR. Start small (inserts/updates on leaves), grow guided by benchmarks.
**R8. Distributed mode correctness:** Leader commit clock default. Sheaf checks + TLA+ export. ECS-native replication. Single-node first (Phase 9 for multi-node).

### Open Questions (§21.1)
Q1. Multi-process writer performance envelope → benchmark SHM vs in-process.
Q2. SSI witness key range/cell refinement → start page-only, refine if abort rate unacceptable.
Q3. Symbol sizing per object type → benchmark, expose PRAGMA overrides.
Q4. Compatibility checkpoint without bottleneck → background checkpoint with ECS chunks.
Q5. B-tree operations for deterministic rebase → inserts/updates on leaf first, grow guided.
Q6. B-link style concurrency for hot-page splits → benchmark, add if internal-page conflicts dominate.

### Future Work
§21.2 Cross-Process MVCC: Phase 6 validates, benchmark mmap TxnSlot vs in-process atomics.
§21.3 WAL Multiplexing: Shard by hash(page_number)%num. 2PC across WAL files. For >100K TPS.
§21.4 Distributed Consensus: Raft/Paxos, WAL = replicated log, RaptorQ log shipping, snapshot shipping.
§21.5 GPU-Accelerated RaptorQ: GF(256) maps to SIMD/GPU, 10-50x for K>10K. wgpu.
§21.6 PMEM VFS: Byte-addressable persistent memory, eliminate WAL, 10-100x latency reduction.
§21.7 Vectorized VDBE: Column-at-a-time, SIMD, 2-5x for analytics. Must maintain trigger semantics.
§21.8 Column-Store Hybrid: Per-column B-trees, RLE/dictionary compression, planner selects.
§21.9 Erasure-Coded Page Storage: Group allocation, .db-fec sidecar, checkpoint-only writer, WAL truncation ordering.
§21.10 Time Travel + Tiered Storage: Retention policy, commit_time metadata, SymbolStore pluggable cold backend.
","created_at":"2026-02-08T05:17:01Z"},{"id":579,"issue_id":"bd-1try","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"### Missing Content: §21.3-§21.10 Future Work (Implementation Notes)

**§21.3 WAL Multiplexing (>100K TPS):**
- Shard WAL frames across multiple files: `hash(page_number) % num_wal_files`
- Each WAL file has own checkpoint state
- Commit requires 2PC across WAL files (atomic append to all touched WAL files)
- Crash recovery: replay prepared-but-uncommitted via global commit marker in primary WAL
- Target: NVMe SSDs with high queue depth

**§21.4 Distributed Consensus Integration:**
- Raft/Paxos for replicated state. WAL entries as replicated log.
- Leader handles writes, followers handle reads (read replicas).
- Snapshot shipping (§3.4.3) for new follower init.
- RaptorQ-coded replication (§3.4.2) for steady-state.
- Challenge: linearizable reads (read from leader or read leases).

**§21.5 GPU-Accelerated RaptorQ:**
- GF(256) maps well to SIMD/GPU. Matrix multiplication embarrassingly parallel.
- Expected 10-50x speedup for large source blocks (K > 10,000).
- Framework: wgpu for cross-platform GPU compute.

**§21.6 PMEM VFS:**
- CXL-attached persistent memory: byte-addressable persistent storage.
- Memory-map DB directly to PMEM. Eliminate WAL (copy-on-write + 8-byte atomic pointer swings).
- Use clflush/clwb for cache line persistence.
- MVCC version chains directly in PMEM with epoch-based reclamation.
- Expected 10-100x latency reduction for small transactions.

**§21.7 Vectorized VDBE Execution:**
- Column-at-a-time processing for SIMD utilization.
- Better CPU cache behavior. Expected 2-5x for analytical queries.
- Challenge: maintain row-at-a-time semantics for triggers and RETURNING clause.

**§21.8 Column-Store Hybrid:**
- Column groups in separate B-trees per column.
- Automatic materialization of frequently-scanned columns.
- RLE + dictionary compression for low-cardinality columns.
- Query planner selects row-store or column-store.
- Challenge: consistency under concurrent writes.

**§21.9 Erasure-Coded Page Storage (normative implementation notes):**
- Modified page allocation: allocate G pages as a group.
- Repair storage: ECS (Native) or `.db-fec` sidecar (Compat).
- Read: try source page first, fall back to erasure recovery.
- Benchmark G=32, G=64, G=128 for space/recovery trade-off.
- **Checkpoint-only writer:** In Compat mode, `.db-fec` maintained only by checkpoint (never by txn writers) to avoid group-level write contention.
- **WAL truncation ordering:** RESTART/TRUNCATE checkpoints must not discard WAL unless `.db-fec` updated + fsync'd for affected groups. Degrade to non-truncating checkpoint if behind.

**§21.10 Time Travel Queries + Tiered Symbol Storage (normative implementation notes):**
- Retention policy: time travel meaningful within configured history window. GC/compaction free to drop old history unless retention pins it.
- Addressing: stable coordinate = commit_seq. Timestamp APIs need commit_time metadata + time→commit_seq index (deterministic virtual time under LabRuntime).
- SQL surface: FOR SYSTEM_TIME AS OF (§12.17) + AS OF COMMITSEQ <n>.
- Tiered SymbolStore: pluggable with optional cold backend (object storage). Remote fetch requires RemoteCap (§4.19.1) + caching + prefetching for predictable latency.

1. **test_risks_have_unique_ids**: R1..R8 unique; Q1..Q6 unique; no renumbering without explicit note.
2. **test_each_high_priority_risk_has_signal**: For each risk with an empirical component (abort rate, overhead, growth), ensure a detection signal exists (benchmark/test/e-process).
3. **test_each_risk_has_mitigation_pointer**: Where applicable, link each risk to at least one mitigation bead (or explicitly mark “unassigned”).

## E2E Review Script (Planned)

- **e2e/risk_register_report.sh**:
  - Emits a summary table of risks/questions.
  - Emits “missing signal” and “missing mitigation” lists.
  - Intended to run periodically to keep the register current.

## Logging Requirements

- Report script emits JSONL lines per risk: `risk_id`, `title`, `signals`, `mitigations`, `status`.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Risk register is actionable: every risk has either a mitigation plan or an explicit rationale for deferral.
- The report script can be used to prevent the risk register from becoming stale narrative.

 {"id":"bd-1tup","title":"§3 RFC 6330 Conformance Test Suite + HDPC Matrix Verification","description":"## SUMMARY

Verify asupersync's RFC 6330 (RaptorQ) implementation at the mathematical level, covering GF(256) arithmetic tables, systematic index table lookups, LDPC/HDPC constraint generation, and encode/decode roundtrip correctness. Per spec §3.1-3.3: FrankenSQLite's durability, replication, and self-healing all depend on asupersync's production-grade RaptorQ codec. While FrankenSQLite does not reimplement RFC 6330, it MUST verify the codec's correctness at the mathematical building-block level because all storage integrity guarantees flow from it. Key verification targets: GF(256) field with irreducible polynomial 0x11D and generator g=2, the worked example 0xA3 * 0x47 = 0xE1, HDPC matrix construction over GF(256) (not GF(2)), systematic index table (RFC 6330 Table 2, K_max=56403), and decode success probability boundaries (K: ~99%, K+1: ~99.99%, K+2: ~99.99999%).

## KEY DATA STRUCTURES / ALGORITHMS

- **GF(256) field** (§3.2.1): Galois Field with 256 elements, irreducible polynomial p(x) = x^8 + x^4 + x^3 + x^2 + 1 (0x11D), generator g = 2. Addition is XOR. Multiplication via OCT_LOG/OCT_EXP tables (766 bytes base) or MUL_TABLES (64KB, 256x256).
- **OCT_LOG / OCT_EXP tables** (§3.2.1): OCT_LOG[a] = k such that g^k = a (for a != 0). OCT_EXP[k] = g^k. Extended to 510 entries to avoid modular reduction. Multiplication: `OCT_EXP[OCT_LOG[a] + OCT_LOG[b]]`.
- **MUL_TABLES** (§3.2.1): Precomputed 64KB table `MUL_TABLES[a][b] = a * b` in GF(256). O(1) single-lookup multiplication.
- **Systematic index table** (§3.2.5, RFC 6330 Table 2): Maps K to K' (smallest supported K' >= K) with associated parameters J(K'), S(K'), H(K'), W(K'). K_max = 56,403.
- **LDPC constraints** (§3.2.3): S rows, each source column j has exactly 3 nonzeros at positions b, (b+a)%S, (b+2a)%S where a = 1 + floor(j/S), b = j%S. Plus S x S identity block.
- **HDPC constraints** (§3.2.3): H rows of dense GF(256) coefficients from the product MT * GAMMA over GF(256). MT is H x (K'+S), GAMMA is (K'+S) x (K'+S) using alpha (primitive element). Plus H x H identity block. These provide the algebraic strength distinguishing RaptorQ from Raptor (GF(2)).
- **Constraint matrix A** (§3.2.3): L x L matrix where L = K' + S + H. Rows: S LDPC + H HDPC + K' LT constraints. Must be invertible for valid K' values.
- **Inactivation decoder** (§3.3): Two-phase: peeling (remove degree-1 rows) then Gaussian elimination on inactive subset.

## NORMATIVE INVARIANTS

1. **INV-GF256-CORRECT**: OCT_LOG, OCT_EXP, and MUL_TABLES MUST match RFC 6330 §5.7 definitions exactly. Any discrepancy means all RaptorQ operations produce wrong results.
2. **INV-SYSTEMATIC-TABLE**: K' lookups MUST return the correct smallest K' >= K from RFC 6330 Table 2. Wrong K' means wrong matrix dimensions and decode failure.
3. **INV-HDPC-GF256**: HDPC rows MUST use GF(256) coefficients (not GF(2)). At least one HDPC entry must be outside {0, 1}. This is what makes RaptorQ superior to Raptor.
4. **INV-L-FORMULA**: L = K' + S + H MUST hold for all valid K' values. This is the total intermediate symbol count.
5. **INV-DECODE-PROBABILITY**: Decode with exactly K symbols MUST succeed >= 99% of the time. Decode with K+2 symbols MUST succeed >= 99.99999% of the time (§3.1).

## UNIT TEST REQUIREMENTS

- **test_gf256_log_exp_tables_roundtrip**: For every nonzero a (1..=255): assert `OCT_EXP[OCT_LOG[a]] == a`. For every k (0..=254): assert `OCT_LOG[OCT_EXP[k]] == k`. Verify `OCT_EXP[0] == 1` and `OCT_EXP[255] == OCT_EXP[0]`.
- **test_gf256_multiplication_worked_example**: Assert `mul(0xA3, 0x47) == 0xE1`. Verify `OCT_LOG[0xA3] == 91`, `OCT_LOG[0x47] == 253`, `(91+253) % 255 == 89`, `OCT_EXP[89] == 0xE1`. Also: `mul(a, 1) == a` for all a; `mul(a, 0) == 0` for all a; `mul(a, inverse(a)) == 1` for all nonzero a.
- **test_gf256_field_axioms**: For 1000 random triples (a, b, c): verify associativity `mul(mul(a,b),c) == mul(a,mul(b,c))`, distributivity `mul(a, a^b) == mul(a,a) ^ mul(a,b)`, commutativity `mul(a,b) == mul(b,a)`.
- **test_gf256_mul_tables_consistency**: Verify `MUL_TABLES[a][b] == log_exp_multiply(a, b)` for all 65,536 pairs (exhaustive).
- **test_systematic_index_table_lookups**: For K in [1, 5, 10, 50, 100, 1000, 10000, 56403]: look up K' and verify K' >= K and K' is in RFC 6330 Table 2. Verify specific values: K'=6 for K=5, K'=10 for K=10, K'=101 for K=100.
- **test_ldpc_constraint_structure**: For K'=10: construct LDPC rows. Verify each source column j (0..K'-1) has exactly 3 nonzero entries. Verify stride formula. Verify S x S identity block present.
- **test_hdpc_matrix_dimensions_and_gf256_entries**: For K'=10: construct HDPC rows. Verify H rows with (K'+S) GF(256) coefficients from MT*GAMMA. Verify H x H identity block. Verify at least one entry is not in {0, 1} (proving GF(256), not GF(2)).
- **test_encode_decode_roundtrip_multiple_k_values**: For K in [4, 10, 50, 100, 500]: encode K source symbols, decode with exactly K received symbols, verify exact match. Then decode with K+2 symbols and verify > 99.999% success rate across 1000 trials.
- **test_parameter_l_equals_kprime_plus_s_plus_h**: For each K' in the systematic index table: compute S(K'), H(K'), verify L = K' + S + H.

## E2E TEST

- **test_e2e_rfc6330_conformance_vectors**: Run RFC 6330 conformance vectors (or equivalent known-good fixtures) end-to-end through asupersync's encoder and decoder. Verify the tuple generator, systematic index, LDPC/HDPC matrix construction, and decode success at the K, K+1, and K+2 boundaries. On mismatch, log the first divergent intermediate artifact (matrix row, symbol payload, or tuple output) and the random seed for reproduction.

## Logging Requirements

- INFO: vector run summary: `vector_id`, `k`, `overhead`, `result`.
- ERROR: on mismatch, log the first divergent intermediate artifact (matrix row, symbol, or tuple output) and the seed.


## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA

- [ ] GF(256) OCT_LOG/OCT_EXP/MUL_TABLES verified against RFC 6330 §5.7 definitions (exhaustive 65,536-pair check)
- [ ] Worked example 0xA3 * 0x47 = 0xE1 verified with intermediate values (logs 91, 253; sum 89; exp 0xE1)
- [ ] GF(256) field axioms (associativity, distributivity, commutativity, inverse) verified for 1000+ random triples
- [ ] Systematic index table lookups correct for representative K values including K_max=56,403
- [ ] LDPC constraint structure verified: 3 nonzeros per source column with correct stride formula
- [ ] HDPC matrix verified: correct dimensions, GF(256) entries (not GF(2)), identity block present
- [ ] Encode/decode roundtrip succeeds for K in [4, 10, 50, 100, 500] with exact-K and K+2 symbol counts
- [ ] L = K' + S + H identity verified for all K' values in the systematic index table","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-08T06:48:05.920856085Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-09T03:14:13.034573719Z","closed_at":"2026-02-09T03:14:13.034543453Z","close_reason":"26 tests GREEN covering all acceptance criteria: GF(256) tables (exhaustive 65536-pair consistency), worked example 0xA3*0x47=0xE1, field axioms (1000 triples), generator order 255, inverse properties, LDPC circulant structure (3 nonzeros per column), HDPC GF(256) entries (INV-HDPC-GF256), L=K+S+H identity (K=1..500+large), S-is-prime (K=1..1000), encode/decode roundtrip (K=4,10,50,100,500), erasure recovery, systematic emission, determinism, RFC 6330 rand/deg bounds, E2E conformance vectors.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1tup","depends_on_id":"bd-1hi","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-08T21:54:22Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1tup","depends_on_id":"bd-1hi.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-08T21:54:22Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":581,"issue_id":"bd-1tup","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"# §3 RFC 6330 Conformance Test Suite + HDPC Matrix Verification

## Scope

This bead covers the conformance testing of asupersync's RaptorQ implementation against RFC 6330, focusing on the mathematical building blocks that FrankenSQLite depends on for correctness: the systematic index table, LDPC/HDPC constraint generation, GF(256) arithmetic, and parameter calculation.

While FrankenSQLite uses asupersync's production-grade RFC 6330 implementation (not a re-implementation), we MUST verify its correctness at the mathematical level because FrankenSQLite's durability, replication, and self-healing all depend on it.

## Spec References

- §3.1: K_max = 56,403; T = page_size; failure probability at K is ~1%, at K+1 ~10^-4, at K+2 ~10^-7
- §3.2.1: GF(256) with irreducible polynomial p(x) = x^8 + x^4 + x^3 + x^2 + 1 (0x11D), generator g = 2
- §3.2.1: OCT_LOG/OCT_EXP tables (766 bytes base), MUL_TABLES (64KB), worked example 0xA3 * 0x47 = 0xE1
- §3.2.3: LDPC constraints: 3 nonzeros per source column j with stride a = 1 + floor(j/S)
- §3.2.3: HDPC constraints: MT matrix * GAMMA matrix over GF(256), H rows providing algebraic strength
- §3.2.3: LT constraints via Tuple function, RaptorQ degree distribution (§5.3.5.4 of RFC)
- §3.2.5: Systematic index table (RFC 6330 Table 2): precomputed K' values with J(K'), S(K'), H(K'), W(K')
- §3.3: asupersync modules: gf256.rs, linalg.rs, systematic.rs, decoder.rs, proof.rs, pipeline.rs

## Requirements

### RFC 6330 Test Vector Verification
1. Verify GF(256) arithmetic tables (OCT_LOG, OCT_EXP, MUL_TABLES) match RFC 6330 §5.7 definitions exactly
2. Verify the worked example: 0xA3 * 0x47 = 0xE1 (OCT_LOG[0xA3]=91, OCT_LOG[0x47]=253, (91+253) mod 255 = 89, OCT_EXP[89]=0xE1)
3. Verify GF(256) field properties: a + a = 0 (XOR), a * inverse(a) = 1 for all nonzero a, associativity, distributivity

### Systematic Index Table Lookups
4. For a set of representative K values (1, 5, 10, 50, 100, 1000, 10000, 56403), verify K' lookup returns the correct smallest K' >= K from RFC 6330 Table 2
5. Verify J(K'), S(K'), H(K'), W(K') derivations for each K' value match RFC expectations

### LDPC Constraint Generation
6. For K'=10, construct the LDPC rows and verify: each source column j has exactly 3 nonzeros at positions b, (b+a)%S, (b+2a)%S where a = 1 + floor(j/S), b = j%S
7. Verify the S x S identity block in the LDPC region

### HDPC Matrix Verification
8. For small K' values (6, 10, 20), construct the HDPC rows and verify:
   - MT matrix dimensions: H x (K'+S)
   - GAMMA matrix dimensions: (K'+S) x (K'+S)
   - Product MT * GAMMA yields H rows of GF(256) coefficients
   - H x H identity block in the HDPC region
9. Verify GAMMA matrix structure uses alpha (primitive element of GF(256)) as specified in RFC 6330 §5.3.3.3

### Parameter Calculation
10. Verify L = K' + S + H for multiple K' values
11. Verify that the constraint matrix A is L x L and invertible for valid K' values

## Unit Test Specifications

### Test 1: `test_gf256_log_exp_tables_roundtrip`
For every nonzero element a (1..=255): assert OCT_EXP[OCT_LOG[a]] == a. For every k (0..=254): assert OCT_LOG[OCT_EXP[k]] == k. Verify OCT_LOG[0] is sentinel, OCT_EXP[0] == 1, OCT_EXP[255] == OCT_EXP[0].

### Test 2: `test_gf256_multiplication_worked_example`
Assert mul(0xA3, 0x47) == 0xE1. Also verify: mul(a, 1) == a for all a, mul(a, 0) == 0 for all a, mul(a, inverse(a)) == 1 for all nonzero a.

### Test 3: `test_gf256_field_axioms`
For a random sample of 1000 triples (a, b, c): verify associativity of multiplication (mul(mul(a,b),c) == mul(a,mul(b,c))), distributivity (mul(a, a^b) == mul(a,a) ^ mul(a,b)), commutativity (mul(a,b) == mul(b,a)).

### Test 4: `test_gf256_mul_tables_consistency`
Verify MUL_TABLES[a][b] == log_exp_multiply(a, b) for all 65536 pairs.

### Test 5: `test_systematic_index_table_lookups`
For K in [1, 5, 10, 50, 100, 1000, 10000, 56403]: lookup K' and verify K' >= K and K' is in RFC 6330 Table 2. Verify K'=6 for K=5, K'=10 for K=10, K'=101 for K=100.

### Test 6: `test_ldpc_constraint_structure`
For K'=10: construct LDPC rows. Verify each source column j (0..K'-1) has exactly 3 nonzero entries. Verify the stride formula: a = 1 + floor(j/S), positions at b, (b+a)%S, (b+2a)%S where b = j%S. Verify S x S identity block.

### Test 7: `test_hdpc_matrix_dimensions_and_gf256_entries`
For K'=10: construct HDPC rows. Verify H rows, each with (K'+S) GF(256) coefficients from MT*GAMMA product plus H x H identity. Verify at least one entry is not in {0, 1} (proving GF(256) is used, not GF(2)).

### Test 8: `test_encode_decode_roundtrip_multiple_k_values`
For K in [4, 10, 50, 100, 500]: encode K source symbols, receive exactly K symbols, decode, verify exact match. Then receive K+2 symbols and decode, verify success rate is > 99.999%.

### Test 9: `test_parameter_l_equals_kprime_plus_s_plus_h`
For each K' in the systematic index table: compute S(K'), H(K'), verify L = K' + S + H. Verify L matches the expected total intermediate symbol count.

 {"id":"bd-1tvfd","title":"WASM 2.8: Security hardening — CSP headers, CORS, encryption key handling, WASM memory isolation","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-02T05:02:15.846459523Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-02T05:02:34.459484766Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1tvfd","depends_on_id":"bd-7l31v","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-08T21:54:22Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1tvfd","depends_on_id":"bd-yqvrr","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-08T21:54:22Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":583,"issue_id":"bd-1tvfd","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"# Task 2.8: Security Hardening for Browser WASM

## What
Address browser-specific security concerns for running a database engine in WASM.

## Areas

### 1. Content-Security-Policy for WASM
Browsers may block WASM execution without proper CSP:
```
Content-Security-Policy: script-src 'self' 'wasm-unsafe-eval';
```
Document required CSP directives. Provide a Next.js middleware example.

### 2. CORS and Cross-Origin
- WASM binary loading requires proper CORS headers
- OPFS is origin-scoped (different origins cannot access each other's OPFS)
- Document CORS requirements for CDN distribution

### 3. Encryption Key Handling
FrankenSQLite supports XChaCha20-Poly1305 encryption. In the browser:
- Keys MUST NOT be stored in localStorage (XSS-accessible)
- Use Web Crypto API for key derivation (SubtleCrypto.deriveKey)
- Consider: keys in IndexedDB with Web Crypto wrapping, or derived from user password via Argon2 in WASM
- Clear keys from WASM linear memory after use (zero-fill)

### 4. WASM Memory Isolation
- WASM linear memory is isolated per instance (browser guarantees this)
- However, JavaScript can read/write WASM memory directly
- Sensitive data (encryption keys, user data) in WASM memory is accessible to any JS on the same origin
- Mitigation: use COOP/COEP headers to prevent cross-origin scripts from accessing the page

### 5. SQL Injection in Browser Context
- Parameterized queries are the ONLY safe way to pass user input
- Document that string interpolation of user input into SQL is forbidden
- TypeScript types should make parameterized queries the easy path

### 6. Side-Channel Attacks
- WASM execution timing is observable from JavaScript
- SharedArrayBuffer + Atomics provides high-resolution timing (Spectre concerns)
- Mitigation: COOP/COEP headers (already required for SharedArrayBuffer)

**Proof Artifacts:** Benchmark output showing p99 non-regression and total throughput increase under adversarial load.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"FuchsiaMink","created_at":"2026-03-04T23:27:04.149210777Z","created_by":"unknown","updated_at":"2026-03-23T06:01:04.415034725Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1uguv","depends_on_id":"bd-3t52f","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-08T21:54:22Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":2513,"issue_id":"bd-1uguv","author":"ubuntu","text":"## DRO Controller Handoff for bd-1uguv (from pane that landed bd-3t52f live controller)

### Unresolved Assumptions in Current Controller

1. **Window budget = 128 decisions.** Not profiled. If real workloads produce 10K decisions/sec, windows rotate ~78/sec. If workloads produce 10 decisions/sec, adaptation takes ~13 seconds. Neither extreme is validated.

2. **Threshold = 0.45 is arbitrary.** The `default_threshold` in `DroLiveControllerConfig` was carried forward from the original static matrix. No evidence it's correct for any workload. The Bayesian-optimal threshold from `LossMatrix` is `L_fp / (L_fp + L_miss) = 1/1001 ≈ 0.001`, which is 450x smaller. The 0.45 value effectively means "never abort on DRO alone" for most occupancy levels.

3. **CVaR penalty formula saturates at low occupancy.** `dro_cvar_penalty(r, w, cert)` = `radius * min((r+w)/8, 4) * ln(1 + max/min)`. At (1,0): tail_mass = 0.125, skew = ln(2) ≈ 0.693, penalty = radius * 0.087. With typical radius ~0.05-0.15, penalty ≈ 0.004-0.013. This never exceeds threshold=0.45. The controller only bites at high occupancy (r+w >= 8).

4. **Edge rate normalization clamps to [0,1].** `DroWindowSnapshot::edge_rate()` = `min(edges/total, 1.0)`. If there are 5 edges per decision on average, edge_rate = 1.0 always. The variance of a clamped signal is artificially suppressed. Pane 5 should test whether edge_rate variance tracks contention changes or is always pinned at 1.0 under realistic loads.

5. **No hysteresis.** The controller computes a new matrix every window rotation. If workload alternates calm/storm on a per-window boundary, the matrix oscillates. No damping or exponential smoothing.

### Workload-Shift Cases to Test

| Case | Calm Phase | Storm Phase | Expected Behavior |
|------|-----------|-------------|-------------------|
| Sustained storm | - | 20 writers, 5 hot pages, 50% abort rate | Radius expands → penalties increase → DRO gates more T3 pivots |
| Calm after storm | 4 writers, 100 pages, 0% abort | (preceding storm) | Radius contracts as old storm windows slide out |
| Zipf switching | Uniform key access | Zipf(1.5) on 3 hot keys | Abort rate variance spikes → radius expands |
| Oscillating | Alternating calm/storm every 128 decisions | Same | Hysteresis gap visible — matrix oscillates with no damping |
| Cold start | First 512 decisions of a fresh controller | - | min_samples=4 means first 3 windows use the default conservative matrix |

### p99 Success/Fail Criteria

- **Throughput:** DRO policy must yield >= static-threshold throughput on the mixed_read_write benchmark at c4 and c8. Regression > 3% is a fail.
- **Abort rate:** Under adversarial Zipf-switching (case 3 above), DRO abort count must be strictly lower than static threshold abort count over 1000+ decisions.
- **p99 latency:** Under sustained storm, p99 commit latency must not exceed 2x the p99 under calm. The DRO controller adds ~25ns per decision (matrix lookup). If p99 degrades beyond 2x, the abort policy is too aggressive.
- **False positive rate:** On uniform key access (no actual anomalies), DRO should produce zero additional aborts vs static threshold. Any false positive increase is a regression.

### Key Code Seams for the Adversarial Test

- `ssi_anomaly_tests.rs` has the `StressTxn` pattern and `MockActiveTxn` infrastructure. Extend it.
- `DroLiveController::global()` is the singleton. The adversarial test can't easily reset it between phases (OnceLock). Options: (a) test with a fresh `DroLiveController::new()` per test, (b) compare abort counts between phases within a single controller lifetime.
- `record_dro_commit/abort()` in `ssi_validation.rs` is the feed point. Every `ssi_validate_and_publish` call already feeds it via `record_evidence_decision`.
- `DroLiveController::generation()` tells you how many matrix swaps have occurred — use it to assert adaptation happened.

### What NOT to Do

- Don't serialize writers to make the test pass. Concurrent mode stays ON.
- Don't lower the threshold to make DRO "work" — that's calibration fraud.
- Don't compare against a no-DRO baseline by disabling the controller — the controller is always active now. Compare against the static initial matrix (generation=0) behavior.

 {"id":"bd-1ukm6","title":"CTE SELECT * column resolution fails with no such column","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-02-27T22:59:51.794637768Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-27T23:00:13.968610853Z","closed_at":"2026-02-27T23:00:13.968592458Z","close_reason":"Fixed: resolve_cte_column_names_with_schema expands * and table.* in CTE column inference","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"comments":[{"id":584,"issue_id":"bd-1ukm6","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## CTE SELECT * Column Resolution Bug — Fixed

### Problem
`WITH eng_emps AS (SELECT * FROM employees WHERE dept = 'eng') SELECT name FROM eng_emps ORDER BY salary DESC`
failed with: `Internal("no such column: name in table eng_emps")`

### Root Cause
`infer_select_column_names()` returns the literal string `"*"` for `ResultColumn::Star` and `"table.*"` for `ResultColumn::TableStar`. When used as CTE column names, these placeholder strings don't match actual column lookups.

### Fix
Added `resolve_cte_column_names_with_schema()` in connection.rs that expands `*` and `table.*` placeholders to actual column names by looking up referenced tables in the schema. Applied to both non-recursive CTE path (line ~8258) and recursive CTE path (line ~8340).

Helper functions added:
- `resolve_cte_column_names_with_schema()` - main resolver
- `extract_from_table_names()` - extracts table names from SelectCore FROM clause
- `collect_table_names_from_source()` - recursive table name collector

### Tests
Added `conformance_026_cte_basic` test that exercises this exact scenario. All 6 conformance_026 tests pass.

### Files Changed
- `crates/fsqlite-core/src/connection.rs` — CTE fix + 3 helper functions
- `crates/fsqlite/src/lib.rs` — 20 new conformance tests (suites 024-026)
- `crates/fsqlite-harness/conformance/024_string_functions.json` — new
- `crates/fsqlite-harness/conformance/025_expression_operators.json` — new
- `crates/fsqlite-harness/conformance/026_subquery_and_cte.json` — new

 {"id":"bd-1uzb","title":"E2E: File Format Compatibility Test Suite (C SQLite Round-Trip)","description":"Unit Test Requirements:
- test_bd_1uzb_unit_compliance_gate: concrete unit identifier for structure lint compliance.
- prop_bd_1uzb_structure_compliance: concrete property identifier for structure lint compliance.

Cross-cutting E2E test suite verifying FrankenSQLite can read/write standard .sqlite files that C sqlite3 can also read/write.

COVERS: §1.1 (Behavioral Parity) + §11 (File Format) + §17.7 (Conformance Testing)

## TEST SCENARIOS

### Scenario 1: Read C SQLite Files
- test_e2e_read_csqlite_created_db: Open a .sqlite file created by C sqlite3, read all tables, verify data
- test_e2e_read_csqlite_with_indexes: Read DB with multiple indexes, verify index-assisted queries work
- test_e2e_read_csqlite_with_triggers: Read DB with triggers, verify trigger metadata preserved
- test_e2e_read_csqlite_all_page_sizes: Test page sizes 512, 1024, 2048, 4096, 8192, 16384, 32768, 65536
- test_e2e_read_csqlite_with_wal: Open DB in WAL mode created by C sqlite3, verify WAL recovery works

### Scenario 2: Write Files Readable by C SQLite
- test_e2e_write_db_readable_by_csqlite: Create DB with FrankenSQLite, open with C sqlite3, verify data
- test_e2e_write_wal_recoverable_by_csqlite: Write WAL frames, verify C sqlite3 can recover them
- test_e2e_write_header_bytes_match: Compare database header (100 bytes) byte-for-byte with C sqlite3 output

### Scenario 3: Format Edge Cases
- test_e2e_varint_encoding_matches_csqlite: All varint encoding edge cases (1-byte through 9-byte) match exactly
- test_e2e_overflow_pages_match_csqlite: Large BLOBs/TEXT values use overflow pages identically
- test_e2e_freelist_pages_match_csqlite: After DELETE, freelist structure matches C sqlite3
- test_e2e_autovacuum_pointer_map: Auto-vacuum pointer map pages match C sqlite3 layout
- test_e2e_record_format_all_serial_types: All 13 serial type codes encode/decode correctly

### Scenario 4: Encoding
- test_e2e_utf8_text_roundtrip: UTF-8 text (including multibyte) survives round-trip
- test_e2e_utf16le_utf16be_databases: Read/write UTF-16LE and UTF-16BE encoded databases

## LOGGING REQUIREMENTS
- Log every header field read/written with expected vs actual values
- Log page reads with page number, type, cell count
- Log varint encoding/decoding: value, byte length, raw bytes
- Log any format divergence from C sqlite3 with exact byte offset

- [ ] Any .sqlite file created by FrankenSQLite can be read by C sqlite3 3.52.0
- [ ] WAL files are cross-compatible between C sqlite3 and FrankenSQLite
- [ ] Database header (100 bytes) is byte-identical for equivalent databases

 {"id":"bd-1uze","title":"PARITY-EPIC: Restore SQLite Observable Equivalence for INTEGER PRIMARY KEY / rowid semantics","description":"Program-level remediation to eliminate current parity failures caused by rowid/IPK semantic drift across INSERT, WHERE/filter fast paths, RETURNING, transaction verification queries, and concurrent-write logical equivalence checks.","design":"Context and root-cause hypothesis
- Current parity failures reproduce in: `fsqlite::tests::probe_insert_returning_rowid`, `correctness_mixed_dml::mixed_dml_delete_then_reinsert`, `correctness_transactions::{txn_rollback_discards_all, txn_multiple_savepoints_rollback_middle}`, `correctness_concurrent_writes::{2,4,8}_threads_disjoint_keys`, and `validation::tests::test_multiple_tables`.
- These failures cluster around one semantic contract: SQLite `INTEGER PRIMARY KEY` (IPK) columns are aliases of rowid at observable query boundaries.
- In current VDBE codegen, `INSERT ... VALUES` and `INSERT ... SELECT` still emit `NewRowid` even when explicit IPK values are supplied, causing rowid to diverge from user-visible `id`.
- Some WHERE/ORDER BY optimized paths resolve IPK identifiers through `Column(idx)` instead of `Rowid`, creating inconsistent behavior between fast paths and generic expression paths.

Program strategy
1. Capture an explicit failure matrix and minimal repros so every fix is tied to evidence.
2. Repair INSERT rowid binding semantics first (highest leverage root cause).
3. Repair WHERE/sort/scan resolution for IPK references next.
4. Add targeted regression tests for every observed failure shape.
5. Re-run full parity matrix and only close when evidence is attached.

Out-of-scope for this epic
- Changing concurrent-writer default behavior (must remain ON by default).
- Re-introducing SQLite-style serialized writer locks.","acceptance_criteria":"1. All known parity failures listed above pass without test mutation that weakens assertions.
2. Explicit IPK inserts are observable as matching rowid values in RETURNING, WHERE id predicates, ORDER BY id, and state hashes/checksums.
3. Concurrent logical-equivalence test (`correctness_concurrent_writes`) matches C SQLite exact `(id,name,val)` tuples for disjoint key ranges.
4. Regression coverage is added so future codegen fast paths cannot silently regress IPK/rowid semantics.
5. `cargo check --all-targets`, `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`, and `cargo fmt --check` pass after remediation.","notes":"This epic is specifically parity-first: correctness before throughput claims. Performance/reliability benchmarking tasks are intentionally separate and must consume this parity baseline as a prerequisite.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-12T01:46:38.193842371Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-12T02:55:33.418991482Z","closed_at":"2026-02-12T02:55:33.418972226Z","close_reason":"PARITY EPIC COMPLETE. Root cause: codegen_insert_values/select ignored ctx.rowid_alias_col_idx. Fix: IsNull/Copy routing for user-supplied IPK with NULL fallback to NewRowid. All 4 phases (A/B/C/D) closed. 10/10 failures resolved. 6,483 tests pass. 13 new regression tests.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"comments":[{"id":588,"issue_id":"bd-1uze","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"# Root Cause Analysis Complete (QuietBeaver, Session 22)

## Executive Summary

Deep investigation has identified a **single root cause** for 9 of the 10 current test failures:
`codegen_insert_values()` and `codegen_insert_select()` in `crates/fsqlite-vdbe/src/codegen.rs`
have access to `ctx.rowid_alias_col_idx` (the IPK column index) but **never use it**. They
unconditionally call `NewRowid` to auto-generate a rowid, ignoring user-supplied IPK values.

## Current Failure Inventory (10 failures across 7 test targets)

| # | Test Target | Test Name | Root Cause |
|---|-------------|-----------|------------|
| 1 | `fsqlite --lib` | `probe_insert_returning_rowid` | IPK value 42 ignored, gets rowid=1 |
| 2 | `fsqlite-core --lib` | `test_group_by_rowid_alias_variants` | GROUP BY on IPK with wrong rowids |
| 3-5 | `fsqlite-e2e --test correctness_concurrent_writes` | `*_2/4/8_threads_disjoint_keys` | All rows get wrong IDs in comparison |
| 6 | `fsqlite-e2e --test correctness_mixed_dml` | `mixed_dml_delete_then_reinsert` | Reinsert id=1 becomes id=2 |
| 7-8 | `fsqlite-e2e --test correctness_transactions` | `txn_rollback_discards_all`, `txn_multiple_savepoints_rollback_middle` | ROLLBACK comparison fails (likely IPK-caused) |
| 9 | `fsqlite-e2e --lib` | `test_multiple_tables` | Validation diff (IPK downstream) |
| 10 | `fsqlite-harness --test rfc2119_hygiene_audit` | `test_beads_use_acceptance_criteria_sections` | Bead metadata (unrelated) |

**9 of 10 failures trace back to IPK.** The 10th is a bead hygiene check (administrative).

## Infrastructure Already Correct

The plumbing is in place — the bug is a **last-mile wiring failure**:

| Component | File | Status |
|-----------|------|--------|
| `ColumnInfo.is_ipk` field | `codegen.rs:21-31` | Correct |
| IPK detection in schema | `connection.rs:1136-1177` | Correct |
| `CodegenContext.rowid_alias_col_idx` | `codegen.rs:85-94` | Correct |
| Context passed to codegen | `connection.rs:3135-3150` | Correct |
| INSERT DEFAULT VALUES | `codegen.rs:1947-1990` | Correct (uses IPK!) |
| SELECT with IPK columns | `codegen.rs:2545-2603` | Correct (emits Rowid) |
| **INSERT VALUES** | **`codegen.rs:2005-2073`** | **BUG: ignores IPK** |
| **INSERT SELECT** | **`codegen.rs:2086-2231`** | **BUG: ignores IPK** |

## The Fix Pattern

Already demonstrated by INSERT DEFAULT VALUES (lines 1947-1990):
```rust
if let Some(ipk_idx) = ctx.rowid_alias_col_idx {
    let ipk_reg = col_regs + ipk_idx as i32;
    b.emit_op(Opcode::Copy, rowid_reg, ipk_reg, 0, P4::None, 0);
}
```

For INSERT VALUES, the inverse: extract the user-supplied value from the IPK register and use it
as the rowid (instead of NewRowid), then copy it back to ensure the record is consistent.

## ROLLBACK Hypothesis

The 2 ROLLBACK failures (`txn_rollback_discards_all`, `txn_multiple_savepoints_rollback_middle`)
are **most likely caused by IPK**, not by a broken ROLLBACK mechanism. Evidence:
- The undo mechanism in `engine.rs:390-479` is correctly implemented
- Tests that DON'T use IPK tables (`test_begin_rollback_restores_state`) PASS
- The e2e framework compares FrankenSQLite vs C SQLite output — IPK mismatches cascade to all
  operations including post-ROLLBACK queries

","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:20:09Z"},{"id":1647,"issue_id":"bd-6d9v","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Task: Benchmark — large transaction (100K/1M row single-txn insert)

### What
Measure performance of very large transactions that stress the WAL and page cache.

### Benchmark Design
- Single transaction: BEGIN, INSERT N rows, COMMIT
- N = 100,000 and 1,000,000
- Table with 5 columns: id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT, email TEXT, score REAL, created TEXT
- Row size ~200 bytes → 100K rows ≈ 20MB, 1M rows ≈ 200MB

### Why This Matters
Large transactions exercise:
- WAL growth (C SQLite: WAL file grows until checkpoint)
- MVCC version chain (FrankenSQLite: page versions accumulate)
- Memory pressure (page cache turnover)
- Commit latency (fsync of large WAL vs MVCC commit)

### Metrics
- Total time for INSERT phase
- Commit latency (time from last INSERT to COMMIT completion)
- Peak memory usage during transaction
- WAL file size at commit time (C SQLite) / version chain depth (FrankenSQLite)
- Post-VACUUM database size (should be identical)

### Expected Behavior
- FrankenSQLite may have advantages in commit speed (no WAL checkpoint needed)
- C SQLite may have advantages in insert speed (mature, optimized C code)
- Memory characteristics will differ due to MVCC vs WAL approach

### Acceptance Criteria
- Benchmarks for both 100K and 1M row transactions
- Total time, commit latency, and memory reported
- Both backends produce same final state (logical comparison)
- No OOM or crash on 1M row transaction

 {"id":"bd-6eyrg","title":"EPIC: Hot-Path Simplification — Copy SQLite's Narrow Execution Shape","description":"## Why

Outside the intentional MVCC/RaptorQ differences, the biggest accidental divergence from SQLite is the hybrid runtime. The hot path goes through a very large Connection object that owns fallback state, caches, observability, VTAB machinery, ATTACH delegation, time travel, MVCC registries, GC, and more (connection.rs has 2686+ fields).

SQLite's hot path is tiny and purpose-built: prepare/step/execute is very direct (prepare.c:715, vdbeapi.c:913, vdbe.c:844). FrankenSQLite's path still pays parse cache checks, rewrite, canonicalization, compile cache checks, background-status gates, and fallback decisions around ordinary execution.

The VdbeEngine is also much heavier than SQLite's VM shape: it owns a very large amount of cross-cutting state and clears much of it on every execute/reset.

## Goal

Make the common-case execution path (prepared statement, no schema change, no fallback needed) look as narrow and direct as SQLite's: parse -> compile -> execute with minimal ceremony in between.

## This is P1 not P0

These are meaningful optimizations but they are NOT the concurrent-writer fix. Track C is the concurrent-writer fix. This work helps single-threaded throughput and should be done, but it should not distract from Track C.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-03-19T20:14:28.915725620Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-19T20:14:28.915725620Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["architecture","performance","track-b"]}
-{"id":"bd-6eyrg.1","title":"B1: Separate common execution path from compatibility/fallback routing","description":"## What

Split Connection::execute* into two code paths:
1. FAST PATH: prepared DML/SELECT with no schema change, no fallback needed. Tight sequence: cache lookup -> bind params -> vdbe_execute -> collect results.
2. SLOW PATH: everything else (CTE materialization, view expansion, fallback execution, ATTACH delegation, VTAB wiring).

## Why

Currently every statement passes through the same maze of checks: parse cache check, rewrite/canonicalization, compile cache check, background-status gates, fallback decisions, observability hooks. A simple "INSERT INTO t VALUES (?, ?)" with a prepared statement should not touch any of that.

SQLite's equivalent: if (pStmt->pProgram) { sqlite3VdbeExec(pStmt); return; }

## Key Locations
- connection.rs execute_statement() (~line 5132)
- connection.rs compile_with_cache() (~line 5274)
- connection.rs execute_compiled() (~line 5963)
- execute_prepared_with_params() (~line 5577)

## Approach
Add an early-exit fast path at the top of execute_prepared_with_params():
  if self.schema_cookie_matches() && stmt.program.is_some() {
    return self.vdbe_execute_direct(stmt.program, params);
  }

## Testing Requirements

### Correctness tests (in fsqlite-core #[cfg(test)] or tests/):
1. test_fast_path_simple_insert — prepared INSERT uses fast path (verify via tracing event)
2. test_fast_path_simple_select — prepared SELECT uses fast path
3. test_slow_path_cte — CTE query falls through to slow path
4. test_slow_path_schema_change — DDL invalidates schema cookie, next query uses slow path
5. test_fast_path_after_schema_stabilizes — schema cookie re-matches, back to fast path
6. test_fast_path_parameterized — "INSERT INTO t VALUES (?, ?)" with params uses fast path
7. test_slow_path_view_expansion — query involving VIEW goes slow path

### Performance regression tests:
8. test_fast_path_latency_vs_slow — measure latency of 1000 prepared INSERTs on fast vs slow, assert fast < 2x slow
9. test_no_regression_complex_queries — complex JOINs, CTEs, subqueries still produce correct results (not faster, just not broken)

### Logging:
- tracing::debug!(path = "fast" | "slow", reason, "execute_path_decision") on every execution
- tracing::trace!(schema_cookie, cached_cookie, "schema_cookie_check") on fast path entry

## Not in scope

- Query plan caching (that's B2)

 {"id":"bd-6eyrg.2","title":"B2: Query plan cache in fsqlite-planner","description":"## What

Add an LRU plan cache to fsqlite-planner so repeated SELECT queries with same SQL template don't re-run the full planner every time.

## Current State

Parse cache and compiled cache have been upgraded to LRU with Arc-wrapped entries. But NO plan cache exists — planner recomputes the full query plan on every execution. For multi-table JOINs this is O(2^N) cost estimation.

## Implementation

In crates/fsqlite-planner/src/lib.rs (or new plan_cache.rs):

plan_cache: LruCache<u64, Arc<QueryPlan>>
- Key: hash of (SQL template, schema_cookie)
- Value: Arc<QueryPlan> (shared, not cloned)
- Size: 128 entries (tunable)
- Invalidation: schema_cookie change invalidates all entries (DDL is rare)

Cache sits in the planner and is queried before running the full planning pipeline.

## Expected Impact
- Complex SELECT with JOINs: 5-50x faster planning
- Simple queries: 1.2-1.5x from avoiding recomputation

## Testing Requirements

### Correctness tests:
1. test_plan_cache_hit — same SQL twice, second skips planner (verify via tracing or plan-cache hit counter)
2. test_plan_cache_miss_different_sql — different SQL, both go through planner
3. test_plan_cache_invalidation_on_ddl — CREATE INDEX invalidates cache, next query re-plans
4. test_plan_cache_schema_cookie_key — same SQL on different schema versions produces different plans
5. test_plan_cache_capacity — insert 129 plans into 128-entry cache, verify LRU eviction (oldest evicted, newest retained)
6. test_plan_cache_arc_sharing — two simultaneous queries share same Arc<QueryPlan> without cloning

### Performance tests:
7. test_plan_cache_speedup — 3-table JOIN repeated 100x, measure planning time with and without cache, assert >3x speedup
8. test_plan_cache_no_regression_simple — simple single-table SELECT, cache overhead < 5% vs no cache

### Logging:
- tracing::debug!(sql_hash, hit = true|false, "plan_cache_lookup") on every planning request
- tracing::debug!(sql_hash, evicted_hash, "plan_cache_eviction") on LRU eviction
- tracing::info!(invalidated_count, "plan_cache_schema_invalidation") on DDL

## Depends on: nothing (independent of Track C)","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":360,"created_at":"2026-03-19T20:14:59.208708631Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-23T06:17:36.074366195Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["performance","planner","track-b"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-6eyrg.2","depends_on_id":"bd-6eyrg","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-19T20:14:59.208708631Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":2514,"issue_id":"bd-6eyrg.2","author":"ubuntu","text":"## Correctness Trap Audit for bd-6eyrg.2 (Query Plan Cache)

### Trap 1: Cache Key Must Include More Than (SQL hash, schema_cookie)

The bead specifies key = `hash(SQL template, schema_cookie)`. This is **necessary but not sufficient**. The planner inputs include:

- `TableStats` (row count, page count) — changes with INSERT/DELETE without DDL
- `IndexInfo` (index column lists, uniqueness) — changes only with DDL ✓
- `WhereTerm` classification — derived from SQL, so covered by SQL hash ✓
- `needed_columns` — derived from SELECT list, covered by SQL hash ✓
- `table_index_hints` (INDEXED BY / NOT INDEXED) — **embedded in FROM clause**, covered by SQL hash ✓
- `cracking_hints` — **runtime-adaptive**, NOT in SQL, NOT in schema_cookie ⚠️
- `PlannerFeatureFlags` — **runtime config**, NOT in SQL ⚠️

**Required action:** Either (a) include `PlannerFeatureFlags` and cracking-hint-generation in the cache key, or (b) document that plan cache is invalidated when feature flags change, and that cracking hints bypass the cache.

### Trap 2: TableStats Staleness — Stale Row Counts Produce Wrong Plans

SQLite's planner uses `sqlite_stat1` for cardinality estimates. When rows are inserted/deleted, stats go stale. SQLite re-analyzes on ANALYZE. FrankenSQLite's `TableStats.row_count` and `page_count` feed `estimate_cost()` which drives join ordering.

If the plan cache keys on (SQL, schema_cookie) only, then:
```sql
INSERT INTO big_table SELECT ... FROM source;  -- 1M rows added
SELECT * FROM big_table JOIN small_table ...;   -- cached plan is WRONG
```
The cached plan was computed when `big_table` had 100 rows. Now it has 1M. The join order may be inverted. Schema_cookie doesn't change for DML — only DDL bumps it.

**Options:**
1. Accept staleness (SQLite does — plan stays until ANALYZE or DDL). Document this.
2. Include a generation counter that bumps on ANALYZE. This is what the bead's testing spec test #4 should verify.
3. Include table row-count-magnitude (log2(rows)) in cache key — conservative, some extra misses, but prevents catastrophic misplans.

**Recommended:** Option 1 (match SQLite behavior) with explicit test proving ANALYZE invalidates the cache.

### Trap 3: Schema Cookie Granularity — All-or-Nothing Invalidation

The existing parse cache uses `schema_cookie` as a generation counter and clears the entire cache on any DDL. This is correct for parse+compile caches (DDL can change column names, types, table existence). But for a plan cache, it means:

```sql
CREATE INDEX idx_foo ON unrelated_table(col);  -- bumps schema_cookie
-- ALL cached plans for ALL tables are invalidated
```

This is safe but expensive for workloads with frequent DDL on unrelated tables. SQLite also does this (full schema reload on cookie mismatch). Accept it for now — per-table invalidation is a premature optimization.

**Test requirement:** `test_plan_cache_invalidation_on_ddl` must verify that creating an index on `table_a` invalidates a cached plan for `table_b`. This is NOT a bug — it's the correct conservative behavior.

### Trap 4: Parameterized Queries — Same SQL Template, Different Plans

```sql
SELECT * FROM t WHERE id = ?1;  -- bound to integer → RowidLookup
SELECT * FROM t WHERE id = ?1;  -- bound to text → FullTableScan (affinity)
```

The SQL text is identical. The hash is identical. But the **optimal plan differs by bound parameter type** because SQLite's type affinity rules affect index usability.

In practice, FrankenSQLite's planner doesn't yet consider parameter types for access path selection (it uses column schema types). So this trap is **latent** — not currently exploitable. But if parameter-aware planning is added later, the plan cache MUST be invalidated or keyed on parameter type signature.

**Test requirement:** Add a test that binds the same parameterized SQL with integer and text values and verifies identical plans are produced. This documents the current assumption. If it starts failing, the cache key needs parameter-type awareness.

### Trap 5: IPK Post-Processing Bypass

In `connection.rs:20330`, there's a post-processing step after `best_access_path()` that upgrades `FullTableScan` to `RowidLookup` for INTEGER PRIMARY KEY columns. This happens **outside the planner**.

If the plan cache stores the raw `QueryPlan` from the planner, it stores `FullTableScan`. The post-processing upgrade must still run on cache hits. If the cache is placed AFTER post-processing (at the connection level), then the cache stores the correct plan. If placed inside the planner, the IPK upgrade is lost on cache hits.

**Required decision:** The plan cache should live at the **connection level** (like parse/compile caches), not inside the planner crate. This ensures all post-processing, including IPK upgrade and future rewrites, runs before caching.

### Trap 6: Concurrent Writers and Plan Cache Scope

The parse/compile caches are per-`Connection` (behind `RefCell`, not `Arc<Mutex>`). This is correct because each connection may have a different schema view in MVCC.

The plan cache must also be **per-connection**, not shared across connections. Two concurrent writers may see different schema versions (one before CREATE INDEX, one after). A shared plan cache with schema_cookie keying could serve a stale plan to a connection that committed a DDL change but hasn't bumped its local cookie yet.

**Test requirement:** Test that two connections with different schema states (one has created an index, one hasn't) produce different plans for the same SQL, and neither poisons the other's cache.

### Trap 7: EXPLAIN and EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN Must Bypass Cache

`EXPLAIN` and `EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN` should reflect the current planning decision, not a cached one. If the user runs ANALYZE and then EXPLAIN to verify the new plan, they should see the re-planned version. Either: (a) EXPLAIN always re-plans, or (b) EXPLAIN reads the cache but this is documented.

SQLite re-plans on every `sqlite3_prepare_v2`, so EXPLAIN always shows fresh plans. Match this.

### Summary of Required Tests Beyond Bead Spec

| # | Test | Why |
|---|------|-----|
| T1 | `test_plan_cache_key_includes_feature_flags` | Flag change must miss cache |
| T2 | `test_plan_cache_survives_dml` | INSERT/DELETE doesn't invalidate (matches SQLite) |
| T3 | `test_plan_cache_invalidated_by_analyze` | ANALYZE bumps generation |
| T4 | `test_plan_cache_cross_table_ddl_invalidates` | DDL on table A clears plan for table B |
| T5 | `test_plan_cache_param_type_invariance` | Same plan for int/text params (current assumption) |
| T6 | `test_plan_cache_ipk_upgrade_on_hit` | IPK post-processing runs even on cache hit |
| T7 | `test_plan_cache_per_connection_isolation` | Two connections don't share cache state |
| T8 | `test_plan_cache_explain_reflects_current_plan` | EXPLAIN doesn't serve stale cached plan |

 {"id":"bd-6eyrg.3","title":"B3: Audit rowid seek and covering-index codegen against SQLite","description":"## What

The performance plan shows PK lookups degrading catastrophically:
- 100 rows: 34x slower (11.9us vs 351ns)
- 1K rows: 220x slower (79.5us vs 361ns)
- 10K rows: 1840x slower (756.2us vs 411ns)

SQLite stays ~350-400ns regardless of table size (log N B-tree). FrankenSQLite grows LINEARLY, suggesting a scan instead of a seek.

## Investigation Steps

1. Compile "SELECT * FROM t WHERE id = 42" and dump VDBE bytecode
   - Expected: OpenRead, SeekEq/SeekRowid, Column, ResultRow
   - Bad: OpenRead, Rewind, Column, Eq, Next (full scan with filter)

2. Check VDBE codegen in crates/fsqlite-vdbe/src/codegen.rs:
   - Does WHERE rowid = ? generate SeekRowid?
   - Does WHERE indexed_col = ? generate SeekGe/Found?

3. Check SeekEq/SeekRowid opcode handlers in engine.rs:
   - Do they call cursor.table_seek() (binary search)?
   - Or iterate with cursor.next() until match?

4. Check covering-index opportunities:
   - "SELECT col1 FROM t WHERE col2 = ?" with index on (col2, col1)
   - Should use index-only scan, never touching the table

## Testing Requirements

### Codegen verification tests:
1. test_select_where_rowid_eq_generates_seek — "SELECT * FROM t WHERE rowid = 42" bytecode contains SeekRowid, NOT Rewind+Next
2. test_select_where_pk_eq_generates_seek — same for "WHERE id = 42" on INTEGER PRIMARY KEY
3. test_select_where_indexed_col_eq_generates_found — "WHERE name = 'x'" with index on name emits Found/NoConflict, not scan
4. test_covering_index_avoids_table — "SELECT a FROM t WHERE b = 1" with index on (b, a) emits OpenRead on index ONLY, no table access

### Performance parity tests:
5. test_point_lookup_100_rows_under_50us — SELECT WHERE id=? on 100-row table < 50us (SQLite is 350ns, we target <50us initially)
6. test_point_lookup_1k_rows_under_50us — same on 1K rows, assert still < 50us (log N means roughly same)
7. test_point_lookup_10k_rows_under_100us — on 10K rows, assert < 100us (was 756us)
8. test_point_lookup_100k_rows_under_200us — scaling test at 100K rows
9. test_point_lookup_scales_logarithmically — measure at 100, 1K, 10K, 100K rows, assert ratio 100K/100 < 4x (true log N is ~1.3x per 10x)

### Covering-index test:
10. test_covering_index_performance — "SELECT a FROM t WHERE b = ?" with covering index, measure vs non-covering, assert covering is faster

### Logging:
- Each test logs the VDBE bytecode dump for the query
- Each test logs the actual execution time and row count
- On failure, dump the full query plan and bytecode

## Expected Impact
If codegen is wrong: 10-100x improvement for point lookups. This is the 1840x gap at 10K rows.

 {"id":"bd-6eyrg.4","title":"B4: Split VDBE hot state from cold feature state","description":"## What

VdbeEngine owns too much cross-cutting state. The cold-state tracking (already done) ensures we only clear used subsystems. This task goes further: physically separate hot state from cold state so the interpreter loop carries less baggage in cache.

## Current State

StatementColdState bitfield tracks: AGGREGATES, CONFLICT_TRACKING, ROWSETS, SEQUENCE_COUNTERS, VTAB_CURSORS, WINDOW_CONTEXTS, REGISTER_SUBTYPES, BLOOM_FILTERS. But VdbeEngine struct still owns all inline.

## Changes

1. Move aggregate state, window contexts, bloom filters, vtab cursors, and rowsets into Box<ColdVdbeState> allocated only when the bitfield indicates use.
2. Hot interpreter loop only carries: registers (SmallVec), cursors, pc, program ref.
3. Improves i-cache behavior for common case (simple DML, point lookups).

## Testing Requirements

1. test_simple_insert_no_cold_allocation — prepared INSERT 1000x, verify Box<ColdVdbeState> is never allocated (check via tracing or allocation counter)
2. test_aggregate_allocates_cold_state — SELECT COUNT(*) allocates ColdVdbeState for AGGREGATES
3. test_cold_state_freed_after_statement — aggregate query completes, ColdVdbeState is dropped
4. test_hot_struct_size_under_N_bytes — static_assert or runtime check that hot VdbeState is < 256 bytes (fits in 4 cache lines)
5. test_no_regression_complex_query — complex query using aggregates + windows + vtab still produces correct results
6. test_performance_simple_dml_improved — 10K simple INSERTs, measure throughput, assert >= baseline (no regression from restructuring)
